 {"id":"bd-jt3k","title":"Task: Wire Hostcall Dispatcher into Agent Loop","description":"# Task: Wire Hostcall Dispatcher into Agent Loop

## Objective

Integrate the ExtensionDispatcher into src/agent.rs so that hostcalls are processed during the agent's execution cycle. THIS IS THE CRITICAL INTEGRATION POINT.

## Background

The agent loop (src/agent.rs) orchestrates:
1. Building context (system prompt, messages, tools)
2. Calling provider.stream() to get model response
3. Processing stream events (text, thinking, tool calls)
4. Executing tools and appending results
5. Iterating until done

The extension runtime runs alongside this but is currently disconnected. We need to:
1. Initialize the extension runtime on agent start
2. Process pending hostcalls after each JS tick
3. Handle async hostcall execution without blocking streaming
4. Clean up on agent shutdown

## Current Agent Structure (src/agent.rs)

```rust
impl Agent {
    pub async fn run(
        &self,
        initial_message: Option<&str>,
        on_event: impl Fn(AgentEvent),
    ) -> Result<Vec<Message>> {
        // ... message handling loop
        loop {
            // Build context
            // Call provider.stream()
            // Process events
            // Execute tools
            // [MISSING: Process extension hostcalls]
        }
    }
}
```

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Add ExtensionDispatcher to Agent

```rust
pub struct Agent {
    provider: Arc<dyn Provider>,
    tools: Arc<ToolRegistry>,
    config: AgentConfig,
    // NEW: Extension runtime and dispatcher
    extension_runtime: Option<Arc<PiJsRuntime>>,
    extension_dispatcher: Option<Arc<ExtensionDispatcher>>,
}
```

### 2. Initialize Extensions on Agent Creation

```rust
impl Agent {
    pub async fn new_with_extensions(
        provider: Arc<dyn Provider>,
        tools: Arc<ToolRegistry>,
        config: AgentConfig,
        extension_paths: Vec<PathBuf>,
        session: Arc<dyn ExtensionSession>,
        ui_handler: Arc<dyn ExtensionUiHandler>,
    ) -> Result<Self> {
        // Load extensions
        let runtime = Arc::new(PiJsRuntime::new().await?);
        
        for path in &extension_paths {
            runtime.load_extension(path).await?;
        }
        
        // Create dispatcher
        let dispatcher = Arc::new(ExtensionDispatcher::new(
            runtime.clone(),
            tools.clone(),
            HttpConnector::new(),
            session,
            ui_handler,
            config.cwd.clone(),
        ));
        
        Ok(Self {
            provider,
            tools,
            config,
            extension_runtime: Some(runtime),
            extension_dispatcher: Some(dispatcher),
        })
    }
}
```

### 3. Process Hostcalls in Agent Loop

```rust
impl Agent {
    async fn process_extension_hostcalls(&self) -> Result<()> {
        if let Some(dispatcher) = &self.extension_dispatcher {
            dispatcher.process_pending().await?;
        }
        Ok(())
    }
    
    pub async fn run(...) -> Result<Vec<Message>> {
        loop {
            // ... existing loop body ...
            
            // Process any pending extension hostcalls
            self.process_extension_hostcalls().await?;
            
            // ... continue loop ...
        }
    }
}
```

### 4. Call Points for Hostcall Processing

Hostcalls should be processed at these points:
1. After extension initialization (activate() may make hostcalls)
2. After each tool execution (tools may trigger extension hooks)
3. After processing user input (input hooks may make hostcalls)
4. During idle periods (timer-based hostcalls)

## Async Execution Concerns

**Problem**: Hostcalls may be async (HTTP requests, tool execution) and shouldn't block streaming.

**Solution**: Use asupersync's structured concurrency:

```rust
async fn process_pending(&self) -> Result<()> {
    let requests = self.runtime.drain_hostcall_requests();
    
    // Spawn each hostcall as a concurrent task
    let handles: Vec<_> = requests.into_iter().map(|req| {
        let dispatcher = self.clone();
        asupersync::spawn(async move {
            let outcome = dispatcher.dispatch(req.kind).await;
            (req.call_id, outcome)
        })
    }).collect();
    
    // Wait for all and complete
    for handle in handles {
        let (call_id, outcome) = handle.await;
        self.runtime.complete_hostcall(&call_id, outcome)?;
    }
    
    Ok(())
}
```

## Error Handling

- Hostcall errors should NOT crash the agent
- Errors return as HostcallOutcome::Error
- JS Promise rejects, extension handles error
- Log errors for debugging

## Testing Strategy

1. Unit test: Agent with extensions initializes correctly
2. Unit test: Hostcalls processed after tool execution
3. Integration test: Extension tool call works end-to-end
4. Integration test: Extension HTTP call works
5. Stress test: Many concurrent hostcalls

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bd-389q (ExtensionDispatcher struct)
- Depends on: All hostcall handler tasks
- Blocks: End-to-end extension functionality

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Agent can be created with extension support
- [ ] Extensions loaded and activated on startup
- [ ] Hostcalls processed at appropriate points in loop
- [ ] Async hostcalls don't block streaming
- [ ] Errors handled gracefully

 {"id":"bd-jw5q","title":"Docs: PLAN_TO_COMPLETE_PORT.md - reconcile checklist with reality","description":"## Goal
Update `PLAN_TO_COMPLETE_PORT.md` so it functions as a historical narrative + high-level overview, not a stale checklist.

## Background
The file still contains unchecked tasks that are already complete in code (e.g., tokio/reqwest removal, provider streaming conformance tests). This causes repeated confusion when using it as a “what’s left” list.

## Scope
- Mark clearly which sections are historical and which are still actionable.
- For any “remaining work” items, replace checkbox lists with links to the corresponding bead IDs.
- Remove or annotate any items that are complete in the repo.

## Acceptance
- No unchecked item remains that is already implemented.
- The doc explicitly points readers to Beads (`br list`, `bv --robot-triage`) for the live plan.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss

[ ] Logs include inputs, outputs, timing, and redaction summary; artifacts list is deterministic
[ ] Any new fixtures/golden outputs are deterministic and documented

[ ] Docs/fixtures updated if behavior or UX changes","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T22:03:27.134205216Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T19:28:48.398066423Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T23:23:45.269065672Z","close_reason":"Completed: PLAN_TO_COMPLETE_PORT.md now points to Beads for live plan; removed stale unchecked checklist + fixed tokio/reqwest drift","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-jw5q","depends_on_id":"bd-gor5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T00:00:02Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"bd-jyml","title":"Create extension test priority ranking","description":"Using the API surface matrix, rank all 102 extensions by test priority. Scoring factors:
- Popularity/notability (official examples > Armin Ronacher > popular npm > niche)
- API coverage (extensions exercising more hostcalls get higher priority)
- Complexity tier (simple first, then medium, then complex)
- Dependency count (fewer deps = higher priority for early wins)
- UI dependency (non-UI extensions can be tested first)

Output: a ranked JSON list that downstream tasks use to determine execution order. The first 25 extensions should be testable with minimal infrastructure; the last 25 may need advanced mocks or shims.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T06:12:14.069511919Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T06:38:43.825417773Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T06:38:43.825355797Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-jyml","depends_on_id":"bd-1k8t","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-05T06:21:07.714151989Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-jyml","depends_on_id":"bd-382l","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T06:12:14.079642477Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":282,"issue_id":"bd-jyml","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"COMPLETED: Generated docs/extension-priority.json with 60 extensions ranked by test priority. -{"id":"bd-k7i","title":"Extensions: conformance fixtures + parity report","description":"Background:
- Built-in legacy extensions provide a controlled, known-good subset for early parity.

Steps:
- Identify built-in extensions under legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/.pi/extensions.
- Capture observable behaviors (tools, slash commands, event ordering, connector calls).
- Generate fixtures using the shared fixture schema and normalization rules.
- Run Rust runtime against the same scenarios and produce a parity report.

Logging requirements:
- Per-extension logs must follow the logging spec and include scenario IDs.

Acceptance:
- Built-in extensions pass parity with clear diffs on failure.
- Results feed FEATURE_PARITY.md for extension API coverage.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss
- [ ] Unit tests cover core success/failure + edge cases for this bead
- [ ] Integration/E2E script exercises the end-to-end path (or explicitly marks N/A with rationale) and emits detailed JSONL logs + artifacts per bd-4u9
- [ ] Logs include inputs, outputs, timing, and redaction summary; artifacts list is deterministic
- [ ] Quality gates pass: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test
- [ ] Docs/fixtures updated if behavior or UX changes

 {"id":"bd-k7ke","title":"Message queue: wire steering_mode/follow_up_mode config","description":"# Goal
Wire queue behavior to config settings `steering_mode` and `follow_up_mode` (legacy: `steeringMode`, `followUpMode`).

# Required Behavior
- Supported values:
  - `one-at-a-time` (default)
  - `all`
- Unknown values must fall back to default with a diagnostic.

# Implementation Notes
- `src/config.rs` already contains fields:
  - `steering_mode: Option<String>`
  - `follow_up_mode: Option<String>`
- Provide accessors that resolve defaults and validate values.

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Queue respects settings with no restart needed once persistence is implemented.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss
